content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-5444

November 21, 1980


Furman B. Roberts
City Attorney
City of Orange
P.O. Box 449
Orange, CA 92666

A-80-11-066

Re: Opinion Request
No. 80-069

Dear Mr. Roberts:

Thank you for your letter of October 24, 1980, requesting
an opinion regarding the disqualification provision of the
Political Reform Act. Because your letter raises no substantial
question involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal
opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal
advice will be helpful.

The facts as I understand them are that there is pending
before the City Council and Planning Commission the application
of two developers who wish to convert their apartments to
condominiums. Mayor Pro Tem Don Smith owns converted apartment
rental units throughout the City. None of Mr. Smith's units
are located near the proposed units of conversion. During
council proceeding regarding the applications of the developers
questions were raised by Mr. Jeffrey Burns, a resident of
the City of Orange, as to whether it was appropriate for
Mr. Smith to participate in the proceeding in light of his
interests in real property. The question you have asked the
Commission to consider is whether Mr. Smith must refrain
from participating in council decisions regarding those
applications for condominium conversion that have been submitted.

Government Code Section 87100 requires that Mr. Smith
refrain from participating in those government decisions in
which he has a financial interest. Government Code Section
87103 provides that:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

. . .

8700900S

Furman B. Roberts
November 21, 1980
Page Two


Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000);

. . .

MR. Smith will be disqualified from participating in
hearing the applicants' requests for condominium conversion
if it is foreseeable that his property interests will be
materially affected by the pending applications if they are
granted or rejected by the council. There is nothing in the
facts that have been submitted by you or Mr. Burns that
suggests that Mr. Smith's interests would be so affected.
His apartment units are not near the units proposed for
conversion by the applicants and, in addition, his units are
presently being rented in a market where there is a high
demand for apartments. Unless condominium conversion requests
presently pending before the City will have some precedential
effect, it is not foreseeable that Mr. Smith's interests
will be affected by council decisions regarding them. Therefore,
Mr. Smith is not prohibited by the Act from hearing the
applicants' requests.

Although Mr. Smith is not required to refrain from
participating in decisions regarding the requests of specific
applicants, he may be disqualified from council decisions
that are made to determine the standards or criteria to be
used for evaluating requests for condominium conversion.
This conclusion is reached on the basis that if Mr. Smith
ever wished to convert his rental units to condominiums he
would be bound by those standards established by the council.

I hope that this advice has been helpful. If you wish
to appeal the denial of your opinion request you may do so
pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

If you have any questions, please contact Dwight Dickerson,
an attorney in our Legal Division, or me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Executive Director

TP:DD:plh
cc: Jeffrey Burns
   Dennis Ghan

Request No.80 009
Date Received 10-29-80
Response Due 11/12 80
Proposed Mailing
to Domino 11-7-80

city of orange
orange civic center • 300 east chapman ave
city attorney
(714) 532-0351

post office box 443

October 24, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
Conflicts of Interest Division
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Gentlemen:

I would like to request an opinion concerning an
allegation that Mayor Pro Tem Smith has a conflict of
interest which would prevent him from voting on the question
of the conversion of certain rental apartment units to own-
your-own condominiums.

Enclosed herein is a street map of the City of Orange
on which I have indicated the location of the apartments
which have requested conversion to condominiums. These
requests have been presented to the Planning Commission and
the City Council in the form of Tentative Tract Approval
Applications. The first Tentative Tract No. 10867 consists
of 84 units which is indicated on the enclosed map. The
second application is adjacent thereto and consists of
"Tentative Tract No. 10866 containing 12 units.

At the City Council meeting of Tuesday, October 14,
1980, after public hearings before the Planning Commission
and after several continued public hearings before the City
Council, one speaker, a tenant from a development other than
the subject development seeking conversion to condominium
ownership, spoke and alleged that Mayor Pro Tem Smith owned
rental units in the City of Orange. Indeed, Mayor Pro Tem
Smith does own rental units in the City of Orange and he has
clearly indicated the location of such rental units in Form
721 which he has consistently filed each year with the City
Clerk of the City of Orange. I enclose a copy of his latest
Form 721 filed with our City Clerk. Moreover, I have indi-
cated on the enclosed map the location in the city of rental
units owned by Mayor Pro Tem Smith. Most of these rental
units are duplexes or triplexes. I believe he owns no more
than 4 rental units located on a single parcel.

Fair Political Practices Commission
October 24, 1980
Page two


As a result of this allegation, Mayor Pro Tem Smith
requested that I seek, on his behalf, an opinion from the
Fair Political Practices Commission concerning any conflict
of interest which he might have in voting on the proposed
conversion. As an abstract economic principle, it is true,
as alleged by this non-resident tenant, that the greater the
number of conversions, the less there exists a supply of
rental housing in the City and, therefore, there may be some
tendency to increase the value of such remaining rental
units. This increase in the value of rental units assumes,
of course, that the supply of rental units remains constant.
No one knows when apartment building will begin again with
the same activity that it exhibited in the early 1960's. As
soon as the economy permits such apartment building, rental
units will again be developed.

At the hearing I pointed out that the tenant who was
speaking had a real job on his hands to prove that it was
"reasonably foreseeable" that the conversion would have a
"material financial effect" on the real property rentals
owned by Mayor Pro Tem Smith. Frankly, I did not hear any
such evidence presented by this speaker at the hearing. In
fact, you will notice that I am sending a copy of this
letter to that speaker, as well as to the President of the
tenants association in the subject conversion area to see if
they have any such evidence as to such material effect.

Also enclosed herein you will find a copy of the letter
of June 3, 1980, from your agency to me concerning the
conflict of interest question presented by Orange City
Councilman Fred Barrera. You will notice that in that
opinion you emphasized the importance of the phrases "reason­
ably foreseeable" that there would be a "material financial
effect." Also, I enclose herein some statistics from the
Planning Department of the City indicating the total number
of rental units in the City, as well as some of the major
rentals on the boundary of the City in other cities which
are available for rental. The large industrial plants in
the City are located on the west boundary near the Santa Ana
River. This area is circled in yellow, cross-hatched in
brown. The employees of these plants might be expected to
create a demand for rentals. Now, of course, it is true
that here, as in many other areas of the state, the vacancy
factor is very low. Most of the units are rented. Of
course, these units that are already rented include those
owned by Mayor Pro Tem Smith. Therefore, Mayor Pro Tem
Smith does not need to worry about having enough applicants
to rent his units regardless of any vote he would cast to
approve this conversion since at the present time he appears
to be able to obtain as many tenants as he needs to keep his

Fair Political Practices Commission
October 24, 1980
Page three


rentals occupied. You will notice that the number of rental
units in the City, as indicated by the Planning Department
statistics are considerable, perhaps 11,000 units. Note
also, the statistical material of rental units in other
cities. Anaheim in areas near the boundaries of the City of
Orange has approximately 3,237 Multiple-Family units none of
which have been converted to ownership. Santa Ana has
approximately 2,000 Multiple-Family units in areas near the
City of Orange of which 140 units have been converted to
ownership. This is a total of more than 5,000 rental units
in areas of nearby cities readily accessible to Orange.
When you add Garden Grove's estimated 500 rental units in
areas near the City of Orange, you have at least 16,000 or
more rental units available to tenants. You will notice
also that the number of units so far converted in the City
is rather small in proportion to the total number of units
that exist, perhaps only 1,000 units. The supply-demand
situation at the present time favors landlords who rent
units. If we desire to make more rental units available, it
is clear that we will have to permit the private sector
through free market incentives and profits to produce more
rental units which people can rent if we are to solve the
tenant demand. Neither have recent statewide threats of
rent control been reassuring to developers of residential
rental developments.

I have indicated the location of a small private col-
lege in the City, Chapman College. The housing office
indicates the school has about 1200 students. Six Hundred
and fifty (650) of these students live on campus in dorms or
college owned apartments. The dorms are not full but only
about 85% to 90% occupied. There is a waiting list for
college owned apartments because the rents are less then
market. Of the 550 students who live off campus, probably
half of these live at home and commute. Only 200 to 300
students at most might seek to rent privately owned apart-
ments. Therefore, the student population of the City does
not appear to pose excessive demand for privately owned
rental units.

If the foregoing factors indicate a conflict of in-
terest on the part of councilman owning rental units in the
City, I don't see how anyone who is involved in owning land
as a source of income in the City would ever be able to vote
on matters involving land development or ownership.

Remember too, that conversions of rental units to units
separately owned creates the possibility of home ownership
for a great number of people who cannot afford a single
family house on a separate lot of land. Also, rentals
located in the City are made available by the departure from
such rentals by persons who purchase units so converted to
ownership.

Fair Political Practices Commission
October 24, 1980
Page Four


I would appreciate your prompt attention to this issue
if possible, inasmuch as the matter has been confirmed for
an opinion by your body before the Council will act on this
conversion request. We would like to again set this matter
for hearing in late November or early December. Naturally,
the applicant for the conversion is anxious for a prompt
answer to this question.

I stand ready to answer any further inquiries which you
may have and supply you with any information you need in
order to answer the request by Mayor Pro Tem Smith for an
opinion on this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Furman B. Roberts
City Attorney

FBR:cfk
cc: City Council
City Manager
City Clerk
Director of Planning & Development Services
Mr. Robert Holland, Attorney at Law
Mr. Jeff Burns
Mr. Marvin Marple

sections.conclusion: There is no hi[gh level of] conflict of interest in this matter. Mr. Smith's rental units are not directly affected by the pending conversions because his properties do not appear to have any material financial effect that is reasonably foreseeable from the conversion requests. The City should determine the standards or criteria for evaluating condominium conversion requests and apply them consistently.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Mayor Pro Tem Smith's ownership of rental units in the City of Orange create a conflict of interest that would disqualify him from voting on condominium conversion applications?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mayor Pro Tem Smith does not have a conflict of interest preventing him from voting on the condominium conversion applications because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the conversions would have a material financial effect on his rental properties.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document heavily degraded by OCR with significant text corruption throughout. FPPC response letter is largely illegible in middle sections. Extracted conclusion and question from more readable portions, but completeness uncertain. Contains both incoming request (October 24) and FPPC response (November 21) in single document. References to specific case precedent (Barrera opinion) present but text unclear.

CONCLUSION: There is no hi[gh level of] conflict of interest in this matter. Mr. Smith's rental units are not directly affected by the pending conversions because his properties do not appear to have any material financial effect that is reasonably foreseeable from the conversion requests. The City should determine the standards or criteria for evaluating condominium conversion requests and apply them consistently.

Box 449 Orange, CA 92666 A-80-11-066 Re: Opinion Request No. 80-069 Dear Mr. Roberts: Thank you for your letter of October 24, 1980, requesting an opinion regarding the disqualification provision of the Political Reform Act. Because your letter raises no substantial question involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal advice will be helpful. The facts as I understand them are that there is pending before the City Council and Planning Commission the application of two developers who wish to convert their apartments to condominiums. Mayor Pro Tem Don Smith owns converted apartment rental units throughout the City. None of Mr. Smith's units are located near the proposed units of conversion. During council proceeding regarding the applications of the developers questions were raised by Mr. Jeffrey Burns, a resident of the City of Orange, as to whether it was appropriate for Mr. Smith to participate in the proceeding in light of his interests in real property. The question you have asked the Commission to consider is whether Mr. Smith must refrain from participating in council decisions regarding those applications for condominium conversion that have been submitted. Government Code Section 87100 requires that Mr. Smith refrain from participating in those government decisions in which he has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87103 provides that: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: . . . 8700900S Furman B. Roberts November 21, 1980 Page Two Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); . . . MR. Smith will be disqualified from participating in hearing the applicants' requests for condominium conversion if it is foreseeable that his property interests will be materially affected by the pending applications if they are granted or rejected by the council. There is nothing in the facts that have been submitted by you or Mr. Burns that suggests that Mr. Smith's interests would be so affected. His apartment units are not near the units proposed for conversion by the applicants and, in addition, his units are presently being rented in a market where there is a high demand for apartments. Unless condominium conversion requests presently pending before the City will have some precedential effect, it is not foreseeable that Mr. Smith's interests will be affected by council decisions regarding them. Therefore, Mr. Smith is not prohibited by the Act from hearing the applicants' requests. Although Mr.
embedding.summary: The City of Orange requested FPPC advice on whether Mayor Pro Tem Smith's ownership of rental units in the city created a conflict of interest regarding his potential vote on apartment-to-condominium conversion applications. The FPPC advised that no conflict exists because the financial impact on Smith's rental properties from the conversions is not reasonably foreseeable.
